stocks.note: Numbers as of market close March 31st
stocks.news:
Markets roared back to life after both the U.S. and Iran signaled they may be ready to end the conflict. Major indexes surged across the board — the Nasdaq led with a nearly 4% gain. It was the best single-day rally of 2026.

Oracle (ORCL +6.03%) earned over $6 billion last quarter but is cutting 30,000 workers to funnel more cash into data centers. Meanwhile, Warren Buffett told investors he regrets selling Apple (AAPL +2.90%) — and Iran threatened cyberattacks on U.S. tech giants including Nvidia, Google, and Microsoft as retaliation for the war. Because apparently bombing wasn't enough.

economy.commentary:
Oil rose 60% in March — the biggest monthly spike since 1988. But there may finally be light at the end of the tunnel: both the U.S. and Iran signaled yesterday that they may be ready to wrap things up. No deal yet, but markets loved the vibes. If a ceasefire materializes by April 6, gas could fall to $3.50-$3.75 by May. Bay Area drivers are still paying $6+ a gallon today.

Meanwhile, the jobs picture just got a reality check. The JOLTS report revised 2025 hiring numbers from 584,000 down to just 181,000 — making it the weakest hiring year since the pandemic. Companies are slowing down on hiring and holding onto the people they have.

Consumer confidence beat forecasts at 91.8, but the future outlook is gloomy — people expect the economy to get worse and inflation to spike again. It's the economic equivalent of smiling through gritted teeth.

crypto.commentary:
Google just dropped a bombshell: quantum computers may be able to crack blockchain security sooner than expected. No machine can do it today, but one researcher puts the odds at 1-in-10 by 2032. If that timeline holds, millions of crypto wallets could be vulnerable.

On the policy front, Congress introduced the "Mined in America" bill, which would give U.S. Bitcoin miners discounts on domestically-made hardware. The U.S. already mines more Bitcoin than any other country, but almost every machine doing the work was built overseas. The bill could also pave the way for an official U.S. Bitcoin reserve.

real_estate.homebuilder: Mortgage rates slid to 6.29% — down for the second straight day — but applications fell 10.5% last week as the spring market stays frozen by energy costs and economic uncertainty.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 16-city tracker dropped another 41 units to 2,526 — the second straight week of decline. Buyers are absorbing homes faster than sellers can list them, and the spring thaw hasn't materialized the way it usually does.

📍 Milpitas: Still the hottest segment in the Tri-City area. Townhome pending (34) now exceeds active (26) by an even wider margin than last week. Only 2 new TH listings hit the market — again. If you're a buyer eyeing Milpitas townhomes, expect multiple offers. The city is also asking residents to weigh in on its 2026 Community Survey at milpitas.gov/communityinput to guide budget and service priorities.

📍 Fremont: Inventory continues to tighten — down 7 to 297 total, despite 58 coming-soon listings in the pipeline. Detached homes remain the core of the market with 87 active and 69 pending. Only 18 new listings actually hit this period. On the development front, H Mart's 100,000 sq ft flagship at Pacific Commons is still on track for late 2026 construction.

📍 Union City: Finally got some breathing room — up 2 to 88 total with 4 new listings (vs. just 1 last week). Still the tightest market in the area, but heading in the right direction. The city's BART Mural Project is bringing community-designed art to 9 BART pillars in the Decoto neighborhood alongside Kennedy Park.

📍 Hayward & Newark: Hayward barely moved (-1 to 213) with 18 new listings keeping pace with demand. South Hayward BART station is temporarily closed after a fire damaged equipment. Hayward Restaurant Month is underway — great time to support local businesses. Newark ticked up 1 to 99, and is hosting a Spring Career Fair today at 39399 Cherry St.

On the national front, homeowners are sitting on record levels of equity — but 97% of it went untapped in 2025. Why? They're locked into pandemic-era 3% rates and have zero incentive to refinance. That means fewer move-up buyers entering the market, which keeps inventory tight at the entry level.

The big wildcard: if Iran ceasefire talks succeed, gas prices could drop from $6 to $3.50 by May, easing the economic squeeze that's kept buyers on the sidelines.
